name: Hughes, Ashley M.
recent_1: Ray, C. E., Wilson, G. M., Hughes, A. M., Cunningham Goedken, C., Liu, E. P.-F., Fitzpatrick, M. A., Suda, K. J., Kota, S. M., Nwankpa, C., & Evans, C. T. (2026). Alert fatigue measurement in clinical decision support: a systematic review. Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association. DOI: 10.1093/jamia/ocag064
recent_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes how alert fatigue (dismissal of alerts due to excessive or irrelevant prompts) has been operationally defined and measured across clinical decision support systems, finding substantial inconsistency in metrics. It matters because reliable, comparable measurement of alert fatigue is a prerequisite for designing decision-support that reduces clinician override behavior and preserves the safety value of alerts.
recent_2: Hanks, J. R., Hughes, A. M., Sultana, S., Klute, R., Formella, K., Flynn, C., Wallenfang, A., Krishnakumar, D., Mourad, M., Cho Morse, Y., & Mischler, M. J. (2026). Facilitating communication with children and young adults with special health care needs through a web-based application: Qualitative descriptive study. JMIR Pediatrics and Parenting, 9, e76512. DOI: 10.2196/76512
recent_2_synopsis: Using qualitative methods and a human-centered design approach, this study identified communication barriers experienced by children and young adults with special health care needs and gathered patient, caregiver, and provider feedback to inform a web-based communication tool. It demonstrates how human factors and participatory design can surface usability and usefulness requirements for assistive health technology in a vulnerable patient population.
recent_3: Ray, C., Goedken, C., Hughes, A. M., Wilson, G. M., Hicks, N. R., Fitzpatrick, M. A., et al. (2025). Standard vs. enhanced implementation strategies to increase adoption of a multidrug-resistant organism alert tool: A cluster randomized trial. Frontiers in Health Services, 5, 1566454. DOI: 10.3389/frhs.2025.1566454
recent_3_synopsis: This cluster-randomized trial tested whether adding audit-and-feedback reports and external facilitation (enhanced implementation) improved facility-level adoption of a multidrug-resistant organism alert tool over standard rollout, with registrations rising from 40% at baseline to 75% (standard) and 89% (enhanced). It matters because it provides experimental evidence on implementation-science strategies for embedding infection-control decision-support tools into routine health-system practice.
seminal_1: Hughes, A. M., Gregory, M. E., Joseph, D. L., Sonesh, S. C., Marlow, S. L., Lacerenza, C. N., Benishek, L. E., King, H. B., & Salas, E. (2016). Saving lives: A meta-analysis of team training in healthcare. Journal of Applied Psychology, 101(9), 1266-1304. DOI: 10.1037/apl0000120
seminal_1_synopsis: This meta-analysis of 129 studies quantified the effectiveness of healthcare team training across all four Kirkpatrick criteria (reactions, learning, transfer, results; d = .37 to .89) and linked training to improved patient outcomes such as length of stay and mortality. With approximately 642 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is Hughes's most-cited work and foundational because it provided the definitive empirical case that structured team training improves both team and patient-safety outcomes, anchoring a decade of healthcare-teamwork intervention research.
seminal_2: Salas, E., Grossman, R., Hughes, A. M., & Coultas, C. W. (2015). Measuring team cohesion: Observations from the science. Human Factors, 57(3), 365-374. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815578267
seminal_2_synopsis: This influential review critiques the inconsistent definition and measurement of team cohesion and recommends modeling it with task and social subdimensions measured through both behavioral and attitudinal operationalizations. With approximately 471 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it imposed conceptual and methodological order on a fragmented cohesion literature and gave researchers and practitioners concrete guidance for valid measurement in team-effectiveness studies.
expertise_overview: Hughes applies human factors and organizational-psychology methods to study teamwork, team training, and coordination in healthcare with the goal of improving care quality and patient safety. A central thread is team training and its evaluation, including meta-analytic synthesis of training effectiveness, training sustainment, and the role of the work environment in transfer. She studies shared cognition, communication, and interprofessional collaboration in clinical settings such as labor and delivery, intensive and acute care, COVID-19 frontline work, and the application of evidence-based protocols (e.g., noninvasive ventilation). A growing focus is the design, implementation, and measurement of clinical decision-support and alerting tools, including alert fatigue, audit-and-feedback practices, and infection-control and antimicrobial-stewardship technologies. Methodologically she combines experimental and quasi-experimental studies, meta-analysis, qualitative and human-centered design, systematic review, and implementation-science trial designs. Her application domains span primary care, infection prevention, surgical/anesthesia safety, and pediatric special-needs care.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Ashley M. Hughes, Department of Biomedical and Health Information Sciences, College of Applied Health Sciences, University of Illinois Chicago, director of the SAFE-T (Systems-based Approach For Enhancing Teamwork) in healthcare lab; PhD trained in the Eduardo Salas lab (University of Central Florida). Sources confirming identity: UIC AHS faculty directory page (ahs.uic.edu/biomedical-health-information-sciences/directory/hughes-ashley-m/), her Google Scholar profile (user=z38zD6MAAAAJ, listing the four anchor Human Factors papers and the Salas-lab co-authorships), and the UIC Cancer Center / MetroHealth Population Health Research Institute profiles. Disambiguation: the seminal 'Saving lives' (J. Applied Psychology, 2016) and 'Measuring team cohesion' (Human Factors, 2015) papers are by this same Ashley M. Hughes (co-authored with Salas and Grossman), not a different Hughes. Citation counts are approximate Google Scholar values retrieved 2026-06-17: 'Saving lives' ~642; 'Measuring team cohesion' ~471; total cited-by ~3,074; counts may drift over time. All 6 DOIs cited in this profile (3 recent: 10.1093/jamia/ocag064, 10.2196/76512, 10.3389/frhs.2025.1566454; 2 seminal: 10.1037/apl0000120, 10.1177/0018720815578267; plus reuse of pre-verified Human Factors DOIs as anchors) were verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17, each returning HTTP 200 with matching titles. The four pre-verified Human Factors anchor DOIs were also re-confirmed HTTP 200. No DOIs were invented and no unverifiable citations remain.
